---
title: "The validation harness"
subtitle: "T6 · Phase T6.3 · `va-harness`"
---

::: {.callout-note}
**Status: partial, but rung 1 is real.** `golden/divider.golden` is genuine QSPICE output, and
`cargo xtask validate` is green against it — the project's first formally passed ladder rung.
Everything else in `golden/` is still empty, for concrete, diagnosed reasons — see "Limitations."
:::

## Goal

Turn "the simulator produced some numbers" into "the simulator produced the *right* numbers":
solve a circuit through the real pipeline, diff it against a committed reference, and report a
clear pass/fail — the one thing that actually earns a ✅ on the ladder-rung table, per
`CLAUDE.md` §7.

## Where it fits

`va-harness` sits at the very end of the pipeline `CLAUDE.md` §2 draws, downstream of
everything else:

```
Verilog-A source          circuit netlist
       │                         │
  [va-frontend] ──IR──► [va-codegen]      [va-netlist]
                              │                │
                        ModelInstance (Interface β)
                              │                │
                              ▼                ▼
                         [va-core] ──► [va-cli] ──► [va-harness] ──► golden/
                                                          │
                                                     (QSPICE, offline,
                                                      as the oracle)
```

It doesn't add a new capability to the simulator itself — it drives the *existing* `va-cli`
pipeline and checks the answer, the same way a test suite doesn't make the code faster, it makes
you trust that it's correct.

## The idea

Three pieces, matching `docs/validation.md` §7:

1. **A metric** — how far apart are "what we computed" and "what's golden"? `va_harness::metrics`
   has two: `max_relative_error` (DC — the worst relative deviation across every point) and
   `rms_error` (transient — root-mean-square over an already-aligned waveform).
2. **A golden format** — a file format to *store* that reference. `va_harness::golden` has two so
   far: `GoldenDc` (one operating point) and `GoldenSweep` (a `.dc` sweep, one `GoldenDc`-shaped
   row per swept value).
3. **A driver** — something that actually runs the pipeline, builds a comparable result, and
   applies the metric. `va_harness::dc` does this for both golden shapes; `cargo xtask validate`
   is the command-line entry point that ties it to a curated list of circuits, and `cargo xtask
   gen-golden` is the entry point that *produces* a golden file in the first place.

QSPICE is the oracle throughout (`CLAUDE.md` §7): we are not building on it, only checking
against it.

### The DC metric, precisely

```
rel = max_i |got_i - ref_i| / max(|ref_i|, floor)
```

`floor` (`1e-12`) exists purely so a golden value of exactly `0` — a diode's leakage current at
`V=0`, say — doesn't turn a negligible absolute difference into a division-by-zero blowup. It's
a division guard, not a "this is a small error" threshold in its own right; a genuinely large
absolute difference against a near-zero reference still divides out to something huge and fails
the check, exactly as it should.

### The golden formats

A `GoldenDc` (`.golden`) is as plain as it gets — and `golden/divider.golden` is a real one,
committed exactly as shown:

```text
in 1
mid 0.5
```

A `GoldenSweep` adds one header line naming the swept source and the node order, then one row
per point:

```text
V1 in
0 0
0.1 0.1
```

Both round-trip exactly through `f64`'s `Display` (Rust's shortest-round-trippable-decimal
formatting, not a truncated one) — `render()` then `parse()` always reproduces the original
value bit-for-bit, which matters for a format whose entire job is being a trustworthy reference.

### QSPICE's own `.qraw` format

QSPICE writes its results as `.qraw`: an ASCII header (`Title:`/`Plotname:`/`No. Variables:`/a
`Variables:` block listing `<index>\t<name>\t<unit>`/`Binary:`), then one little-endian `f64`
per variable — the same header-then-binary-payload shape ngspice's own `.raw` format uses.
Confirmed empirically, not assumed: running `circuits/divider.net` through QSPICE **completely
unmodified** (this project's own SPICE-flavored `.net` syntax already parses as a QSPICE deck)
gives `V(in)=1`, `V(mid)=0.5` — bit-for-bit this project's own already-known answer.

## The code

`va_cli::load` — parses a netlist and, if given, compiles a `.va` model through the real
frontend → codegen pipeline. `run_sim` (the CLI's own entry point) already did this inline;
it's now its own `pub fn` so `va-harness` can reuse it instead of re-implementing netlist parsing
or shelling out to the CLI binary and re-parsing stdout:

```rust
pub fn load(netlist: &str, model: Option<&str>) -> Result<(Netlist, Vec<Module>)>
```

`va_cli::solve_dc` / `solve_dc_sweep` — likewise made `pub`, unchanged otherwise.

`va_harness::dc::run_dc` — the glue: load, solve, package as a `GoldenDc`.

```rust
pub fn run_dc(circuit: &str, model: Option<&str>) -> Result<GoldenDc, HarnessError> {
    let (net, compiled) = va_cli::load(circuit, model)...?;
    let op = va_cli::solve_dc(&net, &compiled)...?;
    Ok(GoldenDc::from_operating_point(&net.node_order, &op.x))
}
```

`va_harness::dc::compare_dc` — diff two `GoldenDc`s. Node order must match exactly first — a
silent value-by-position diff across differently-ordered nodes would compare unrelated
quantities and could pass by accident:

```rust
pub fn compare_dc(got: &GoldenDc, golden: &GoldenDc) -> Result<Verdict, HarnessError> {
    if got.node_order != golden.node_order {
        return Err(HarnessError::NodeOrderMismatch { .. });
    }
    let error = metrics::max_relative_error(&got.values, &golden.values)?;
    Ok(Verdict::new(error, tol::DC_REL))
}
```

`run_dc_sweep`/`compare_dc_sweep` do the identical thing for a `.dc` sweep, flattening every
point's node voltages into one long series before handing it to the same `max_relative_error`.

`xtask::gen_golden` — the other direction: for each circuit in `QSPICE_NATIVE_CIRCUITS`, locate
`QSPICE64.exe` (`QSPICE_PATH` env var, then `PATH`, then the standard install location), run it
on a scratch copy of the deck, parse the `.qraw` (`parse_qraw`), look up each of this project's
own `node_order` names by their `"V(name)"` label (`golden_dc_from_qraw` — QSPICE's own variable
ordering isn't assumed to match `node_order`'s), and write the resulting `GoldenDc` to
`golden/<name>.golden`.

`cargo xtask validate` walks two small, explicit tables (`DC_CIRCUITS`, `SWEEP_CIRCUITS`) — one
entry per circuit this project currently knows how to golden-check — and for each: if
`golden/<name>.golden` exists, run + compare + report `PASS`/`FAIL`; if it doesn't, report `skip`
rather than failing the build.

## It works

The comparison logic itself — metrics, golden parse/render round-trips, `.qraw` parsing (against
a synthetic fixture built to match a real QSPICE run byte-for-byte in shape),
node-order/length-mismatch rejection — is unit-tested without needing QSPICE running at test
time:

```console
$ cargo test -p va-harness --lib
running 28 tests
...
test result: ok. 28 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out; finished in 0.00s

$ cargo test -p xtask
running 9 tests
test tests::parse_qraw_reads_a_divider_style_fixture ... ok
test tests::find_qspice_finds_the_real_install_on_this_machine ... ok
...
test result: ok. 9 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out; finished in 0.00s
```

Generating the golden reference for real:

```console
$ cargo run -q -p xtask -- gen-golden
[xtask] gen-golden: regenerating golden/ from QSPICE …
[xtask]   using C:\Program Files\QSPICE\QSPICE64.exe
[xtask]   wrote .../golden/divider.golden (2 node(s))
[xtask] gen-golden: 1 circuit(s) regenerated from QSPICE (of 3 known — the rest need a
native-model translation and/or a temperature-convention fix, see QSPICE_NATIVE_CIRCUITS's doc
comment)
```

And validating against it:

```console
$ cargo run -q -p xtask -- validate
[xtask] validate: running va-harness over the model zoo vs golden/ …
[xtask]   PASS circuits/divider.net: error=0.000e0 (tol 1e-4)
[xtask]   skip circuits/mos_dc.net: no golden reference at .../golden/mos_dc.golden
[xtask]   skip circuits/diode_iv.net: no golden reference at .../golden/diode_iv.golden
[xtask] validate: 1 checked, 0 failed, 2 skipped (no golden)
```

Exit code `0`, and `error=0.000e0` — QSPICE and this project's own pipeline agree exactly on a
resistor divider. A deliberately-wrong golden value reports `FAIL` and a non-zero exit instead,
confirmed the same way (plant a wrong `mid` value, re-run, restore it).

## Limitations

**Only `circuits/divider.net` has real golden data.** It's the sole circuit that's both a pure
`R`/`C`/`V` deck (so QSPICE's own built-in primitives apply with zero translation) and free of
temperature-sensitive nonlinearity. `mos_dc.net`/`diode_iv.net` each use a custom `.va` model
(`models/mosfet.va`/`diode.va`); regenerating their golden needs an equivalent QSPICE-native
`.model` card for each, *and* resolving a real, empirically-diagnosed mismatch: QSPICE's default
simulation temperature is 27°C (300.15 K), while this project's codegen fixes a single constant
pair (`va_codegen::TEMP=300.0`, `VT=0.025_852`, exactly 300 K) for every model's `$vt`. That
0.15 K difference is invisible for a linear circuit but produces a real ~0.85% divergence in
`diode.va`'s exponential I–V law at a representative bias point — past `DC_REL`'s `1e-4`
tolerance. Forcing QSPICE's `.temp` to exactly 300 K does **not** close the gap; it opens a
*different* one, since standard SPICE diode models rescale `IS` relative to their own nominal
temperature (`TNOM`) whenever `.temp` differs from it — confirmed by trying it and getting a
*worse* mismatch, not a better one. `docs/roadmap.md`'s T6.3 section has the full numbers.

**No `golden/*.golden` file is fabricated, ever, for any circuit** — `CLAUDE.md` §7's "QSPICE is
the oracle" rule means golden data must come from an *independent* simulator, not from this
project's own hand-derived math, even when that math is verifiably correct. A `golden/` file
with a hand-computed value in it would quietly stop being a check on correctness and start being
a check on arithmetic consistency with itself.

**DC only.** `rms_error` (the transient metric) is implemented and unit-tested, but nothing
feeds it a real waveform yet — that needs a `GoldenWaveform` format and a "shared-timebase
resample" step (two different transient runs won't land on the same timepoints unless one is
resampled onto the other's), neither written yet. `.tran` circuits (`rc_step.net`,
`rectifier.net`) aren't in any `xtask validate` table for this reason.

**A per-rung/convergence-fraction dashboard** (§7's fourth metric, "fraction of zoo circuits
that reach a solution") doesn't exist — `validate`'s own pass/fail/skip tally is the closest
thing today.

## What's next

1. Resolve the 300 K vs 300.15 K thermal-voltage convention (most likely: move this project's
   own fixed constants to the 300.15 K SPICE-standard value) — the real blocker for
   `mos_dc.net`/`diode_iv.net`'s golden data, once QSPICE-native `.model` equivalents for
   `mosfet.va`/`diode.va` exist too.
2. `GoldenWaveform` + `rms_error`'s shared-timebase resample, to bring `.tran` circuits into
   `xtask validate`.
3. With rung 1 now genuinely ✅, the next milestone is rung 2 or 5 doing the same — worth its own
   worked example once the temperature convention is settled.
